frameworks.name: VSL Pre-Frame Close System
frameworks.definition: A three-part cold-outbound close architecture: (1) VSL that pre-frames the prospect before the call, (2) scripted qualification and pre-frame on the call, (3) training cadence ensuring reps follow the process — designed to move close rates from 6% to 20%+ on cold leads.

frameworks.name: Quality + Quantity Offer Bifurcation
frameworks.definition: Split a project-based service into two distinct offer tracks: a high-ticket upfront quality package capturing representative ICPs, and a lower-cost recurring quantity package providing fresh, ongoing UGC-style assets — enabling continuity revenue that project-only pricing cannot sustain.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that applying a warm sales process to cold leads is the primary reason cold-outbound close rates are low. A warm process assumes a buyer who is already in a buying cycle and requires little pre-framing; cold leads have no prior context, no trust, and no established intent, so the same process under-converts by roughly two-thirds relative to a properly built cold process.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: cold outbound leads with no prior relationship
sales call as primary conversion vehicle
claims.mechanism: Cold leads lack context and trust; a warm process skips the pre-framing steps that cold leads need to become qualified buyers, so the close fails at the awareness-mismatch stage.
bounded_by: Does not apply to inbound or warm referral leads who are already in their buying cycle

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states the rule: build your sales process for cold leads and it will work for warm leads too; build it only for warm leads and it will fail on cold leads. A cold-optimized process includes additional pre-framing and qualification steps that warm leads simply skip through, but those extra steps do not harm warm conversions.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: build for cold and it works everywhere
conditions: applies to any outbound or mixed-source lead environment
claims.mechanism: Cold-built processes add pre-framing and qualification layers; warm leads pass through these layers quickly without friction, preserving their conversion rate.
bounded_by: Does not mean warm-process optimization is wasted — it matters when all leads are warm

claims.text: For a video testimonial agency at $6M revenue booking 100 cold outbound calls per month via LinkedIn ads and named-account targeting, Alex Hormozi diagnosed the close rate at 6% of leads (roughly 8% of the 85% show rate) as a sales-process problem, not a lead-quality problem. The leads were qualified (named accounts) and the show rate was strong (85%), so the loss occurred inside the call itself.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: we just need to build a sales motion
conditions: cold outbound via LinkedIn ads
named-account targeting with incentivized call booking
$15K average order value B2B service

claims.text: Alex Hormozi prescribes a VSL (video sales letter) installed before the sales call as the first fix for cold outbound close-rate failures. The VSL pre-frames the prospect, establishes context and credibility, and begins the qualification process before the rep gets on the phone — shortening the call, improving show-rate quality, and lifting close rates.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: cold outbound leads
scheduled call-based close (not self-serve)
claims.mechanism: A pre-call VSL moves prospects from problem-unaware or solution-unaware to product-aware before they speak to a rep, reducing the warm-up work that kills cold-call conversion rates.
bounded_by: Less necessary for inbound leads who already researched before booking

claims.text: Alex Hormozi prescribes a scripted qualification and pre-frame step on every cold-outbound sales call, installed before the close attempt. Without this step, reps skip straight to presenting, which fails on cold leads who have not yet established they have the problem, the budget, or the urgency.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: set qualification and a pre-frame for the closed call
conditions: cold outbound B2B service calls
reps following a scripted process

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that installing a sales training cadence — a repeating process of monitoring whether reps follow the prescribed script and process — is the third required component after VSL and scripted pre-frame. Without enforcement, reps revert to ad-hoc warm-style behavior even after training.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: training cadence in place for the sales guys
conditions: multi-rep sales team on cold outbound
scripted close process installed

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that improving close rate on cold outbound via VSL pre-framing and scripted process has a compounding effect on sales team utilization: fewer calls needed per closed deal means the same number of reps close more deals, which in turn allows the business to selectively upgrade to higher-quality closers over time.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: virtuous loop rather than ambitious one
conditions: cold outbound B2B service
close rate improvement via process, not headcount addition
claims.mechanism: Higher close rate reduces calls-per-deal, freeing rep capacity, which reduces the required headcount to hit revenue targets and raises the bar for rep quality as lower-performers become redundant.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observes that a 6% close rate on qualified cold outbound leads is not necessarily bad compared to inbound warm leads, because the comparison baseline matters. If warm referral close rates are 40-60% and cold outbound closes at 6%, the gap represents a process problem; if the comparison is other cold outbound benchmarks, 6% may be in range but still improvable.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: I don't necessarily think that's bad if it's compared to somebody who's a warm introduction
conditions: cold outbound calls vs. warm introduction baseline

claims.text: Alex Hormozi diagnoses the lack of continuity revenue in a project-based video testimonial service (average $15K per project, no recurring contract) as a structural offer problem, not a customer behavior problem. Customers buying 3-4 testimonials at a time and then going dormant signals that the recurring need exists but no recurring offer has been designed to capture it.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: I would have assumed this was continuity
conditions: done-for-you service with an ongoing underlying customer need
$9K-$15K per project pricing

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that video testimonials or customer reviews have a shelf-life problem: stories and reviews that are 18+ months old lose persuasive relevance because they signal that the company may no longer be delivering at the same level, even if the underlying story is still valid.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: if you have reviews or testimonial stories that are 18 months old, they lose relevance
conditions: B2B service companies using testimonials in their sales process
claims.mechanism: Stale proof creates a recency gap; prospects infer that current service quality may have degraded if all social proof is old, reducing the trust-transfer value of existing testimonials.
bounded_by: The story itself may still be valid; recency affects perceived current-state quality, not historical accuracy

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends bifurcating a video testimonial offer into two tracks to unlock continuity: a high-ticket upfront quality package (capturing representative ICP avatars using the customer's language, priced at $15K-$25K) and a lower-cost ongoing quantity package (UGC-style snippets and five-star reviews on a recurring basis). The upfront package is the big-head offer; the recurring package is the long-tail continuity revenue.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: done-for-you video testimonial or review service
currently project-based with no recurring contract

anchors.value: ongoing UGC snippets and five-star reviews, lower per-unit cost

examples.text: Alex Hormozi described his own book launch as an illustrative counterexample of warm vs. cold process mismatch. At the book launch, he had a warm audience who already knew him. He could have sent a simple email saying he wrote 17 extra chapters and offering them free with an AI bundle — and estimated he would have converted roughly one-third of sales from that email alone to a warm list. But if he had treated his entire audience (including cold segments) with that same minimal-context email approach, he would have under-converted by approximately two-thirds of what he should have achieved. The warm shortcut only works on the warm slice.

The video testimonial agency in the conversation was at $6M in revenue, selling to mid-market and enterprise B2B software companies (director of marketing level), with a $15K average order value. They had grown historically through inbound and word-of-mouth (high close rates, buyers already in buying cycle). They had recently shifted to cold outbound via LinkedIn ads targeting named accounts, booking 100 calls per month. Of those, 85% showed up. But the close rate was only 6% of total leads booked (approximately 8% of shows). Alex Hormozi identified the root cause immediately as applying the same sales process they used on warm inbound leads to these cold outbound calls, without adding a VSL pre-frame, qualification script, or sales training cadence.

practices.text: Install a VSL before every cold-outbound scheduled call to pre-frame the prospect and begin qualification before the rep gets on the phone.
Build a scripted qualification and pre-frame sequence for the sales call itself, separate from the VSL — run this at the opening of every cold-outbound call before presenting or pitching.
Implement a recurring sales training cadence to verify that reps follow the prescribed cold-call process; monitor call recordings and iterate on the VSL and script based on where prospects drop off.

For a done-for-you service with a recurring underlying customer need, design two offer tracks: a premium upfront package and a lower-cost recurring package — do not rely on project-by-project repeat purchases to create continuity revenue.

Capture representative ICP avatars in the upfront testimonial or proof package using the customers' own language, so the emotional resonance track is complete before launching the ongoing quantity track.
